paper.title: Using bacterial population dynamics to count phages and their lysogens

proof_flows.name: Plate-reader infection curves can be converted into an OD-based phage counting assay with broad dynamic range and single-phage sensitivity.

items.verify: The reproduction loads the microplate infection time series, identifies per-well lysis OD, and writes calibration and blind-test titer tables.
items.paper_reference: Fig.1c; Fig.2a-c; Methods 'Microplate-based infection assay for measuring phage concentrations'
items.expected: Non-empty machine-readable outputs are produced for time-series summaries, detected first local OD maxima for infected wells, fitted calibration data, and OD-based titer estimates for unknown phage samples.

items.verify: The reproduced workflow/summary figure reflects the assay design used in the paper.
items.expected: The workflow shows uninterrupted plate-reader growth with direct phage addition, optional antibiotic selection for lysogen counting, and separate analysis branches for lysis-OD calibration, full-curve model fitting, and lysogen inference by back-extrapolation.

items.verify: Representative infected growth curves have the same overall shape as the paper's examples.
items.expected: Lytic infection curves show initial growth followed by a sharp OD collapse at massive lysis, a pause, and later recovery; the temperate-plus-selection example retains post-infection growth consistent with lysogen survival.

items.verify: The lambda ts calibration dataset spans the reported concentration range used to establish the OD-counting curve.
items.paper_reference: Fig.2a; Methods 'Calibration curves using known phage concentrations'
items.expected: The lambda ts calibration dilution series spans from 6 x 10^2 PFU/mL to 6 x 10^11 PFU/mL.

items.verify: Lysis OD is an approximately linear and monotonic function of the logarithm of the initial lambda ts concentration.
items.expected: When plotted against the logarithm of initial phage concentration, lysis OD forms an approximately straight monotonic line across the calibration series.

items.verify: OD-based phage counts agree in kind with plaque-assay counts across phage types and replicate variability remains modest.
items.expected: OD-based counts for lambda ts, lambda wt, T5, and P1vir cluster near the identity line against plaque counts, and replicate variation remains modest without a clear phage-specific failure mode.

items.verify: The single-phage sensitivity experiment matches the reported inoculum, expected occupancy, observed lysis count, and hypothesis test result.
items.paper_reference: Fig.2e; Methods 'Detecting single phages using the OD-based method'
items.expected: In the single-phage assay, the mean added inoculum is 0.38 PFU per 10 uL aliquot, the expected fraction of wells with non-zero phage is 0.316, the observed lysed count is 10 of 32 wells, and the two-tailed binomial-test p-value is 0.849.
items.tolerance: inoculum ±5%; expected fraction ±0.02; lysed wells ±0; p ±0.05

proof_flows.name: A coupled bacteria-phage dynamical model fits OD trajectories, predicts phage kinetics, and supports growth-rate-dependent lytic infection parameters.

items.verify: The ODE-fitting workflow runs for uninfected growth and infected cultures and produces parameter tables plus simulated trajectories.
items.paper_reference: Fig.3a-e; Methods 'Modeling the OD dynamics of phages and bacteria during infection'
items.expected: Non-empty outputs are produced for uninfected-growth parameterization, null-model and growth-dependent infection-model parameters, and simulated OD/phage trajectories suitable for plotting.

items.verify: The implemented model structure and bacterial-growth parameterization match the paper's description and visual behavior.
items.expected: The model tracks nutrients, uninfected cells, infected intermediates, and free phages, and the uninfected-growth fit reproduces the observed multi-phase OD dynamics with declining inferred nutrient availability.

items.verify: A growth-rate-dependent infection model fits the infected OD data better than the constant-parameter null model.
items.expected: At least one growth-rate-dependent variant closely overlays the infected OD curves, whereas the constant-parameter null model fits appreciably worse.

items.verify: The reproduced r-model captures the infected OD trajectories used in the main text.
items.expected: The fitted r-model reproduces the timing and depth of lysis across the set of initial phage concentrations used for fitting.

items.verify: The inferred model predicts phage concentrations measured from extracted samples over time.
items.expected: Predicted phage trajectories follow the measured extracted-phage time courses in temporal ordering and order of magnitude for the fitted cultures.

items.verify: The relative viral growth parameter increases with host growth rate.
items.expected: The derived relative viral growth parameter shows an upward trend with host growth rate rather than remaining flat or decreasing.

items.verify: The recovery-extension model infers the reported switching rate into phage resistance.
items.paper_reference: Results paragraph on growth recovery; Supplementary Fig.12
items.expected: The inferred rate of switching into resistance is (5.5 +/- 0.9) x 10^-8 per min.

items.verify: The conserved-parameter analysis in minimal media reproduces the reported characteristic growth-rate values.
items.paper_reference: Results paragraph on R; Supplementary Fig.10
items.expected: The fitted characteristic growth rate g* is 0.052 min^-1 in M9Mal and 0.035 min^-1 in M9Glu.

proof_flows.name: A one-cycle infection followed by antibiotic selection can quantify lysogen formation and reveal lysogeny even after single-phage infection.

items.verify: The lysogen-counting workflow runs on infection and detection plate data, back-extrapolates growth curves, and writes lysogeny-versus-MOI results.
items.paper_reference: Fig.4b-c; Methods 'Measuring the frequency of lysogeny as a function of MOI and growth rate'
items.expected: Non-empty outputs are produced for selected-growth fits, extrapolated initial lysogen abundance, extrapolated total-cell abundance, and bulk lysogeny frequency as a function of effective MOI.

items.verify: Temperate and obligately lytic lambda infections show the different collapse depths reported in the paper.
items.expected: At matched high multiplicity of infection, the temperate lambda wt culture shows a shallower population collapse than the obligately lytic lambda ts culture.

items.verify: Under kanamycin selection, infected cultures yield extrapolatable regrowth curves suitable for estimating initial lysogen abundance.
items.expected: Selected infected cultures exhibit delayed exponential regrowth that can be back-extrapolated to a positive initial lysogen abundance, while uninfected selected controls remain flat.

items.verify: The measured lysogeny curve rises with effective MOI and the inferred single-cell response allows lysogeny from single-phage infection.
items.expected: Bulk lysogeny increases with effective MOI, and the inferred single-cell response allows a non-zero lysogenization probability from single-phage infection rather than a strict hard threshold.

items.verify: The survivor population after lambda wt infection is overwhelmingly lysogenic as assessed by kanamycin resistance.
items.expected: The fraction of surviving cells that are kanamycin-resistant lysogens is greater than 99%.

proof_flows.name: Changing host growth physiology shifts the balance of lambda development toward lysogeny in slow-growing cells and increases spontaneous induction in fast-growing lysogens.

items.verify: The growth-rate-dependent lysogeny and spontaneous-induction workflows run successfully and produce fitted parameter tables plus figure-ready summaries.
items.expected: Non-empty outputs are produced for host growth-rate estimation, growth-condition-specific lysogeny fits, fate-diagram predictions, and spontaneous-induction model fits.

items.verify: The host growth-rate profile and sampled infection conditions match the paper's qualitative pattern.
items.expected: Host growth rate decreases as cultures progress to higher OD, and the sampled infection conditions span clearly distinct physiological states from fast growth to near-stationary growth.

items.verify: Bulk lysogeny-versus-MOI curves shift upward as bacterial growth slows.
items.expected: Lower-growth conditions show higher lysogeny frequencies across the MOI range than faster-growing conditions.

items.verify: The inferred single-phage lysogenization probability declines approximately exponentially with growth rate.
items.expected: The inferred single-phage lysogenization probability is highest in slow-growing cells and declines monotonically with increasing host growth rate on a log scale.

items.verify: The fitted two-phage-versus-single-phage lysogenization gain matches the paper's reported magnitude in growing cells and disappears in stationary cells.
items.expected: In growing cells, the ratio of lysogenization probabilities for two-phage versus single-phage infection is 40-80 fold; in stationary cells there is no significant increase.
items.tolerance: growing-cell ratios within 30-90 fold; stationary ratio <=2

items.verify: The combined MOI-by-growth-rate fate diagram has the same directional structure as the paper.
items.expected: Both experimental interpolation and model prediction place the highest lysogeny in the high-MOI, low-growth region and the lowest lysogeny in the low-MOI, fast-growth region.

items.verify: The spontaneous-induction model jointly reproduces the OD and released-phage trajectories from growing lysogens.
items.expected: The fitted model captures both the lysogen OD time course and the extracted-phage time course from the same culture.

items.verify: The inferred spontaneous induction rate spans the reported endpoint values across host growth states.
items.paper_reference: Fig.5f inset; Results paragraph on spontaneous induction
items.expected: The spontaneous induction rate is approximately 0 per min in slow-growing cells with doubling time >= 70 min and rises to about 1.1 x 10^-5 induction events per min in early-exponential cells with doubling time about 30 min.

items.verify: The growth-rate dependence experiment includes the reported number of distinct infection series.
items.paper_reference: Methods 'Infecting at different MOIs and growth rates'
items.expected: The lysogeny-by-growth-rate dataset contains 6 distinct infection series at different host growth rates.
